07 Mapping Your Value Engine - YouTube.mp4
Whether you're mapping a growth engine or a fulfillment engine, the steps are more or less the same. Step one is to define the engine to be mapped. Now we recommend starting with with the growth engine for your best-selling product or service. Remember, it's common for a business to have multiple growth engines, so choose the process that is... responsible for the largest chunk of your sales. For example, if you sell your widget via webinars and trade shows, but webinars make up the larger percentage of your sales, then Map the webinar growth engine for now, and you can do the trade show one later. For step two, you'll need to define the triggering and ending events. Now, as the name suggests, the triggering event initiates the process and the ending event is the final action that concludes the process.
For growth engines, it's common for the triggering event to be a prospect—such as seeing a Facebook, Instagram, or Google ad, or maybe it's a referral, or maybe you have more organic. Kind of whatever that initial point of awareness is, that's going to be your triggering event. And when it comes to the ending event for... growth engine, that's typically a purchase or a closed sale. For fulfillment engines, the triggering event is typically the completed purchase from the related growth engine. So in this way, growth engines kind of flow into fulfillment engines. And the ending event is either the delivery of the product or service, or ideally following up with a customer or client to ensure that they're satisfied. Now, while it may seem simple, don't rush this step.
If you dig a little deeper, you'll often find that what you think is the beginning is actually a few steps into the process. And what you believe is the end is maybe coming up just a few steps short. For example, in one of my companies, our team believes that their job was over, meaning the fulfillment engine was done, when product access was sent to the customer. There was zero follow-up after the initial sale to check that one, if they'd received access, and two, that they were happy with the purchase. When our metrics revealed that this particular product had higher than normal cancellation and refund rates, we decided to map the value engine to see where the problem might be. When we got to this point in the exercise and I asked the team, okay, what's the final action?
Concludes this process, this particular fulfillment engine, someone spoke up and said, sending out the order confirmation and access information. I looked around the room and I saw a lot of smart people nodding their heads in agreement. And it was then when I just about threw up in my mouth. This product was many, many hundreds of dollars, yet... After the sale was closed, we had zero contact with the customer. No, thanks for your order. No, how's it going? And certainly no, is there anything we can do to... help you get even more value from your purchase. Nope. Just thanks for the cash. I'll talk to you again. Probably never. What a horrible customer experience. And worst of all, we know better, right?
This team wasn't made up of a bunch of lazy slackers who don't care about our customers. No, that wasn't the case at all. They just assumed, there's that word by the way, that someone else was handling that particular piece. But— when we map the fulfillment engine, it was clear that the end, the final action, needed to be redefined. The fix was simple, and the results were immediate, but this is why you don't want to skip over this step. Once you know the engine that you're mapping and you've defined the triggering and ending events, you are ready to move on to the next step, which is... Step three, brainstorming tasks and activities between the start and finish. Now, this is the fun part.
Once you have identified— the beginning and the end of your process map— you simply point at the first sticky note and you say, 'Okay, team, this is where we all agree that this value engine starts, right this point here.' But then... What happens? I want you to say it with me again. Then... What happens? This is the only phrase you truly need to know. Then what happens if you have the right people in the room and you ask this question? Your value engines will map themselves as people throughout. Answers, just write them on a sticky note and post it next to the previous step. Occasionally, someone will say, 'Hey, we missed a step.' And when that happens, confer with the room. And if everyone agrees, no problem.
Just move things around and add it in. Other times, someone will say that you missed a step and really you didn't. It's just kind of a piece of an existing step. If this action is important enough, you can choose to break it out into its own step, but I would err on the side of simplicity, meaning less post-it notes, not more. And I find when this happens that just stacking a sub-step underneath the main step, that's usually enough to appease the person who mentioned it, and then you can move on. Another answer that you'll hear to the then what happens?' Somebody will say, 'Well, it depends.' When you hear 'it depends,' that's a clue that you have reached a decision point or gateway and it's time.
Turn the sticky note on a diagonal, magically transforming it from a square into a diamond. This, by the way, is why I really love buying the simple square yellow post-it notes, because they come with both squares and diamonds included in one package. Pretty cool, right? All right, so once you have your diamond, all you need to do then is just to write a description of what might happen with a question mark and use the... of the diamond to denote the different directions that the path could take. For example, when we're mapping growth engines that involve registration pages or other forms of lead capture, it's common to see diamonds with the word 'opt-in'. So 'opt-in' with a question mark or 'register', meaning 'did it happen?' to denote whether or not a given visitor gave us their contact information.
If they did, they can proceed on to the thank you page. If they didn't, they bailed out of the process. Which we can either choose to send them another direction for follow-up, or we can just end the process. Either way, just keep asking, and then what happens? Until you bridge the gap from the start to the end of your value engine. Once you're finished, congratulations! You have mapped your very first value engine. Now, why you have some momentum? Why not map another one?
